name: Why African Central Banks Cannot Do What the Fed Does

chapters.title: HOOK
chapters.scriptText:
In 2021, the International Monetary Fund distributed six hundred and fifty billion dollars in emergency reserves to its member states. Africa received thirty-four billion. Europe received one hundred and seventy billion. Both figures came from the same formula, applied by the same institution, in the same week.
That gap is not the subject of this video. It is the starting point. Because the reason that gap exists — and the reason it matters — runs deeper than any single allocation decision. It goes back to how the global financial system was built, who built it, and what it was built to do. This is a deep dive into the geopolitical history of African economics — specifically, into the structural machinery that means a central bank in Nairobi or Lusaka, no matter how well it is run, cannot respond to a financial crisis the way a central bank in Washington or Frankfurt can. And why that asymmetry was not an accident.

scenes.script: In 2021, the International Monetary Fund distributed six hundred and fifty billion dollars in emergency reserves to its member states.
Africa received thirty-four billion. Europe received one hundred and seventy billion. Both figures came from the same formula, applied by the same institution, in the same week.

chapters.title: ACT I — THE CONVENTIONAL ARGUMENT
chapters.scriptText:
What the Standard Framework Claims
In March 2022, the United States Federal Reserve began the most aggressive interest rate hiking cycle in forty years. Between March 2022 and July 2023, the Federal Open Market Committee raised its benchmark rate eleven times — five hundred and twenty-five basis points in total, from near zero to a range of five-and-a-quarter percent. The stated purpose was to bring inflation down from a forty-year high. By 2024, it had fallen to below three percent. The tool worked.
The conventional explanation for why it worked rests on three pillars. First, the Fed has institutional independence from political interference — which gives its decisions credibility in financial markets. Second, it operates with a clear mandate to control inflation and maximise employment, so markets can anticipate how it will behave. Third, it has access to the full range of monetary tools: interest rate adjustments, forward guidance, quantitative easing, and, when the system is under severe stress, emergency liquidity mechanisms that can be deployed at scale.
These pillars are well-documented in the academic literature. The evidence from the United States, the United Kingdom, and the European Union is genuine. The framework is not a fiction. The argument that any central bank can achieve what the Fed achieves, given the right institutional design and political commitment, is made in serious journals by serious economists. It shapes the conditions attached to IMF loans across Africa. It appears in the policy recommendations of the World Bank. It is, in the mainstream, the default.
The problem with the default is the assumption buried inside it: that every central bank operates in the same structural environment. That assumption is not supported by the data — and the gap between the assumption and the data is not a marginal one.

scenes.script: What the Standard Framework Claims
In March 2022, the United States Federal Reserve began the most aggressive interest rate hiking cycle in forty years.

chapters.title: ACT II — THE THREE STRUCTURAL CONSTRAINTS
chapters.scriptText:
Constraint One: The Currency of the Debt
The United States government borrows in dollars — the currency it issues. When the Fed raises rates, the government pays more to borrow in the same currency it prints. This is painful. It is not existential. The economic pain stays within the system that produced it.
The Central Bank of Kenya does not issue dollars. Kenya's government has borrowed in dollars — through Eurobonds issued on international markets, through multilateral loans from the IMF and World Bank, through bilateral lending agreements. As of 2022, roughly sixty-nine percent of Kenya's external debt stock was denominated in US dollars, according to the Central Bank of Kenya. When the dollar appreciates — as it did sharply during the Fed's 2022–23 hiking cycle — the local-currency cost of that debt rises automatically, regardless of anything the Central Bank does. No rate decision in Nairobi changes what Kenya owes in Washington.
This is what economists Barry Eichengreen and Ricardo Hausmann named the original sin of sovereign borrowing in their influential 2003 paper: the structural inability of most developing economies to borrow long-term in their own currency on international markets. The name captures the logic precisely. The vulnerability is not acquired through any single bad decision. It is inherited from the structural position that developing economies occupy in a dollar-denominated global financial system.

Constraint Two: No Seat at the Backstop
When the global financial system comes under acute stress, the Federal Reserve functions as the dollar lender of last resort — not just for the United States, but for a significant portion of the world. The mechanism is the currency swap line.
The Fed maintains permanent, unlimited swap lines with five central banks: the Bank of Canada, the Bank of England, the European Central Bank, the Bank of Japan, and the Swiss National Bank. These lines allow those central banks to access dollar liquidity directly from the Fed, without burning their own foreign exchange reserves, during periods of dollar shortage. During the 2020 pandemic, the Fed extended temporary swap lines to nine additional central banks — Australia, Brazil, Denmark, South Korea, Mexico, New Zealand, Norway, Singapore, and Sweden — all of which had also accessed the facility during the 2008 financial crisis.
The Central Bank of Kenya does not have a swap line. The Bank of Zambia does not. The Central Bank of Nigeria does not. No African central bank has ever been included in the Fed's swap line network, temporary or permanent. When global dollar liquidity tightens, the Fed's fourteen counterpart central banks can draw on essentially unlimited dollar support. African central banks absorb the shock from their own reserves. Those reserves are finite. The backstop that the richest economies take for granted does not extend to the continent.

Constraint Three: The Capital Flight Transmission
When the Fed raises interest rates, capital flows toward dollar assets — US Treasuries, US corporate bonds, US equity markets. That capital comes from somewhere. A portion of it comes from emerging market and frontier market assets. Investors who held Kenyan government bonds or Nigerian equities sell those positions and move the proceeds into higher-yielding, lower-risk dollar instruments.
The result is a double pressure on African central banks. The exchange rate weakens as capital leaves the country, which raises the local-currency cost of dollar-denominated debt — the original sin problem, activated again. And to slow the capital outflow, the central bank must raise its own rates further — even as higher rates slow domestic growth, raise borrowing costs for local businesses, and increase the government's domestic debt service burden simultaneously. The Fed's tightening cycle does not stay in the United States. It is transmitted, structurally, into economies that had no vote in the decision and no access to the tools that would allow them to absorb it.
Consider what this looked like in practice. When the Fed raised rates by five hundred and twenty-five basis points between March 2022 and July 2023, the Central Bank of Kenya raised its own benchmark rate by six hundred basis points across the same period — from seven percent in May 2022 to thirteen percent by February 2024 — a larger proportional tightening than the Fed itself, deployed to fight the very currency and debt pressures that the Fed’s cycle had amplified. The Kenyan shilling depreciated by nine percent against the dollar in 2022, then by a further twenty-six point eight percent in 2023 — a cumulative loss of roughly thirty-six percent of its value across the hiking cycle. Kenya’s external debt, sixty-nine percent of which was in dollars, became correspondingly more expensive to service in shilling terms — not because Kenya had borrowed more, but because a committee in Washington had voted to raise a rate.

scenes.script: Constraint One: The Currency of the Debt
The United States government borrows in dollars — the currency it issues. When the Fed raises rates, the government pays more to borrow in the same currency it prints.

import React, { useMemo } from 'react' import { useCurrentFrame, interpolate } from 'remotion' export const AnimationComponent = () => { const frame = useCurrentFrame() const title = "Double Pressure on Central Banks" const rawHeads = ["Exchange Rate", "Domestic Rates", "Structural Transmission"] const rawSubs1 = ["Capital flight", "Rate hikes", "Structural"] const rawSubs2 = ["Debt cost rises", "Borrowing costs", "Transmission"] const rawSubs3 = ["Original Sin", "Debt service", "Impact"] const rawTags = ["Currency Weakness", "Capital Outflow", "Growth Slowdown"] const rawPanelColors = ['#0D1B2A', '#F2F2F0', '#FF2222'] const rawHeadColors = ['#FFFFFF', '#FFFFFF', '#FFFFFF'] const items = useMemo(() => { return rawHeads .map((head, i) => ({ head, sub1: rawSubs1[i], sub2: rawSubs2[i], sub3: rawSubs3[i], tag: rawTags[i], color: rawPanelColors[i], hColor: rawHeadColors[i] })) .filter(item => item.head !== '' && item.head !== 'Placeholder') }, []) const count = items.length const titleOp = interpolate(frame, [0, 20], [0, 1], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) const titleTy = interpolate(frame, [0, 20], [20, 0], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) const barW = interpolate(frame, [10, 40], [0, 1920], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) const labelOp = interpolate(frame, [48, 62], [0, 1], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) const panelW = 560 const panelH = 680 const panelY = 180 const gap = 40 const startX = (1920 - count * panelW - (count - 1) * gap) / 2 return (
{title}
{items.map((item, i) => { const x = startX + i * (panelW + gap) const pOp = interpolate(frame, [15 + i * 10, 30 + i * 10], [0, 1], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) const pTy = interpolate(frame, [15 + i * 10, 30 + i * 10], [30, 0], { extrapolateLeft: 'clamp', extrapolateRight: 'clamp' }) return (
{item.head}
{item.tag}
{item.sub1}
{item.sub2}
{item.sub3}
) })}
) } export default AnimationComponent
